config.title: Sixty Days of Standing
config.description: 60 days of 60 minutes of standing meditation.

marks.text: “Nothing is lifting away from the ground.”
“Every part of you is listening to gravity.”

1.caption: Cues I said aloud to myself. (I record these sessions.)

2.title: Day 2 transcript Blackout Poem
2.caption: What remained after the practice log was blacked out.
poem: shoulder blades and arms directly affect my spine
inhabit the whole body all at once
kua sinks, spine lengthens, sternum draping
rising crown, sinking elbows, expanding shoulders
I never felt too far off
left off with 15 minutes to go
body: This is day two of standing practice. Thinking it'll be useful to capture these standing sessions whenever I can. Just because, amongst other things, it's interesting to have a record of what I'm working on and when.
So we begin with five minutes of the small dance.
You know, we're coming into the second five minutes. And for this five minutes, I really want to focus on {foundation} again, the sense of {changing pressure} {within the joints}.

Okay, now we're on number three, on our third round of five minutes. That was interesting. Now I'm going to do a practice of the small dance. But now I just want to pay attention to the {path} that the ankle joints make in space. Knees, hips, pelvis.
Cool. Path {started to become} {initiation}, which was interesting. But now I'm going to take a break and do some rolling fists.

All right, continuing with day two. I just spent -- let's see how long -- 20, 22 minutes doing some rolling fists on the ground. And what I was noticing was this tremendous sense of being able to coordinate, initiate a sequence of movements from within the arms and the {shoulder blades}, and to have my {arms directly affect my spine}. Especially, there seems to be some -- what I would call {dead spaces} or unresponsive spaces -- within the shoulder girdle, connecting into the spine. You know, I'd like to see my shoulder blades and my arms talk more, more intimacy and more clarity to the rest of my torso. So it was really great to get into this.

I think that the rotating wrists for walking lesson could be really valuable to do after exploring rolling fists for a couple of days, because it has a similar function. It just comes at it more through the lens of finding congruent rotation. Sort of coordinating the spine, mostly sagittally. Feels great for my shoulders to move in this way. Yeah, really lovely.
Okay. Let's get into it. Let's do some inhabiting, I think.
So just in a normal stance, a normal posture, {inhabit the whole body all at once}. Just notice what maybe wants a little bit more time, where you don't have as much of...

Feel the sense of self, tune to the sense of self within all of those areas -- gender, sexuality in the pelvis; power in the abdomen; love, tenderness in the chest; voice in the neck; mental activity in the head; knowledge that builds up in the body. Release and know.
The whole body, the walls of my room, sights and sounds -- all of this is permeated by space. Same space permeates the walls, permeates my body. {Same pervasive space}.
And from within that pervasive space, now I'm going to take the Wuji standing posture. We're going to do this for five minutes.
So within that pervasive space, I'm bringing all of the alignment points online.

Okay. {Kua sinks}, {spine lengthens}, tailbone relaxes, hips inflating, kua sinking. Shoulders expanding, {sternum draping}, fingers lively, heavy elbows out of expanding shoulders. Crown suspended, sternum draping.
Releasing pelvis, sinking kua, soft abdomen, crown suspended, expanding shoulders, heavy elbows. {Rising crown}, {sinking elbows}, {expanding shoulders}.
Whoo. That felt really quite good. I kind of lost a little bit of the sense of pervasive space, which I do think could be useful.
Okay, let's do another round. Wuji standing. Maybe a little bit more attention to the pervasive space.

Okay. That's 30 minutes in total now spent in standing. Nice. I still didn't have the full sense of continuity I would have loved with attuning to pervasive space, but {I never felt too far off}, as it were.

It strikes me that the inhabiting practice is pretty good for attuning... or no, the inhabiting practice is pretty good for {dropping interpretation} about the spots that may be held or compressed, collapsed.

For me at least, it can be high friction to kind of go into a practice and just immediately start trying to -- you're operating on an assumption that parts of you are collapsed, which is fine, and that you need to open them up. But that can be a high friction starting place.
But I do feel {nice and open}.

There -- yeah, there's this interesting relationship between the different practices where, from a metasystematic perspective, they have this important balancing function. They move in slightly different directions, but they can work well together. It's the same way of like a handsaw and a sander are fundamentally different -- a planesaw and a sander, they have different functions, but they work well together.

One axis is like endurance versus sensitivity. Different practices have a different relationship to those, but leaning into one or the other can be really useful. There's also something where having awareness principle function can allow you to be both more efficient in your practice, because you can work with difficulties more, with more skill, by changing tack. And it also allows you to... I don't know, I don't know what the next point was. But anyway, cooking, cooking!
Let's do some more standing practice. I'm going to do some air standing.

Crown suspended within space. Every part of my arms holds up the beach ball with exquisite sensitivity. The inner parts of my arms. The sensitivity of my fingertips is present along the inner parts of my arms.
Another reason to keep these is I think I'll come up with interesting practice cues, meta notes on practice that are useful or helpful, things that could be shared.
{Crown suspended} in space. Spine is long, along both the front and the back. Shoulders expand within space.
Okay, taking a break for a moment. Just coming back into Wuji. All the cues happening within expansive space.
This five minutes is wrapping up 20 minutes more. I think we're sitting at 35 minutes total. That will take us to 40.

For this five minutes, I'm going to put the hands overhead in the branching position overhead. Palms oriented upward, little internal rotation in the shoulders. Hands are directly overhead. This is all occurring {within space}. Letting both the front and the back of the spine lengthen down. Pelvis hangs from the lats. Whole spine hangs from the lats -- from the latissimus dorsi, if that's not clear on the transcript.
{Each in-breath softens the lats}. All within space.
Crown suspended.

Question for the large language model: why is it that when I bring my arms overhead, it's -- it's like my neck wants to go into extension and my chin moves forward and up. What is it biomechanically that makes it challenging to maintain length along the back of the neck and the front of the neck at the same time? And biomechanically, what makes it challenging for the sternum to sink down in the same position with the arms up overhead and shoulder flexion?
Okay, three minutes seems to be a good edge right now to hold these positions. Now I need a little bit of a break. So I'm just relaxing back into Wuji standing in pervasive space.
{Dantien area} continues to be interesting to me. It's like {calling} to me -- it feels a bit tense and held. So in future sessions, it might be something to consider.

Okay, we're sitting at 40. I think for now to get to 45, I'm just going to do one Nam'khor movement. Let's do space bear, pervasive space. Finding the same {unmoving space} that pervades all sensation, the {unchanging}. Within any aspect of the coordination, that space remains the same. Within your connection to the ground, that space remains unchanged. Within the rhythm of the breath...

Question for the large language model: so I'm aware of the biomechanical connection when I bring my arms up overhead and how that pulls the pelvis -- or can kind of pull the pelvis into anterior tilt because the lats are having to lengthen. But I'm guessing -- what does the progression look like? What is the intended progression here? Like, what changes physiologically such that that doesn't happen? I imagine some of that is like intramuscular coordination stuff. But then there's also the tissue adaptation. Can you break that down for me cleanly and concisely?
Wowza. Feels great. Okay, {left off} at 45 minutes -- got {15 minutes to go}.

items.title: there's been a lot of grind-set energy lately, so it was lovely to let everything relax for a while.

The [wuji style directions](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A94CfQvbbWM&t=225s) assume a direction and a model of opening and releasing. [Non-directed body movement](https://standing-awareness.com/standing-around) is doing something quite different: it relaxes interpretation, lets me notice that I'm interpreting a perception instead of being with it as it is.

4.today: Cloud cover gathered across the first forty minutes before a bright blue, cloudless final third emerged.

periods.detail: A little bit of thought and a little thickness in the body. Nothing exceptional.
Practice felt fuzzy. Mind busy. Not problematic.
Bright blue sky: ease and enjoyment, clarity, and spaciousness.

4.outlook: Tomorrow: high chance of non-directed body movement, with clearer skies.

sections.heading: Intention
sections.body: This is part art project, part experiment, and part fun n games. I think this might be an interesting artifact for myself and others!

sections.heading: Finding my edge
sections.body: I typically do anywhere from 20 to 40 minutes of standing practice a day. This will definitely push my edge, and force me to prioritize more.

sections.heading: What you'll find
sections.body: Each day has one journal entry, but the format varies widely!

rigorous.body: Long-form journal text. Paragraphs separated by blank lines. Markdown-light: *italic* and **bold** supported.

items.title: Steve Paxton, Material for the Spine

recipe.title: How to stand for sixty minutes

field-notes.subject: Subject 14
field-notes.body: Subject stood for the duration. Right knee released at 0:34:12. No further notable events.

footnote.body: Today's practice referenced¹ several recurring touchstones², and one new one³.
citations: An overheard line from a podcast about geology.

ai-art.prompt: A figure standing in a sun-faded room, wuji posture, 35mm film, soft contrast, the air visible.

blackout.body: I {woke up} early {and tried} to {stand for an hour} but {my} {mind} {kept} {wandering} {to} {the} {coffee} {I had not yet made}.

weather.today: Bright, with passing clouds of impatience. Steady from the south.

mtg.name: Standing Still
mtg.text:
When Standing Still enters the battlefield, take a breath.

Whenever you would do something, consider not doing it.
mtg.flavor: The hardest move is the one you don't make.

transcript.text: Alright. Beginning. Feet on the ground.
Already noticing the left knee wants to lock.
Settling. The shoulder blades are still arguing.

vine.caption: the smallest unit that still shows what it was

handwritten.alt: Photo of a handwritten journal page in cursive ink.

polaroid.caption: where the small dance was loudest

the umber of an old book under the lamp
the chestnut of my own shadow
